coredata.dc:description: In this work, an endogenous self-doping N,P,S-CQDs was synthesized by the acid hydrolysis of pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima) and its performance as a corrosion inhibitor was evaluated. Mild steel (MS) is one of the most used materials in the industry; however, it has low corrosion resistance in acidic environments. Pumpkin is cultivated worldwide, and this compositional characteristic makes pumpkin seeds a great alternative as a raw material to produce CQDs. The results show that endogenous self-doping CQD was successfully obtained with a size distribution smaller than 10 nm. The N,P,S-CQDs presented 91.3% at 60 ppm and 94.6% at 10 ppm as maximum inhibition efficiency after 2 and 24 h immersion, respectively. Activation energy studies, XPS analysis, and isotherm studies show that the inhibition mechanism occurs through physical interaction. These results show an excellent alternative for using pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima), an agroindustry waste, as a corrosion inhibitor.

head.ce:title: Pumpkin (Cucurbita maxima) seed-derived nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur carbon quantum dot as an inhibitor of corrosion for mild steel in HCl solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an endogenous self-doping N,P,S-CQDs was synthesized by the acid hydrolysis of pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima) and its performance as a corrosion inhibitor was evaluated. Mild steel (MS) is one of the most used materials in the industry; however, it has low corrosion resistance in acidic environments. Pumpkin is cultivated worldwide, and this compositional characteristic makes pumpkin seeds a great alternative as a raw material to produce CQDs. The results show that endogenous self-doping CQD was successfully obtained with a size distribution smaller than 10 nm. The N,P,S-CQDs presented 91.3% at 60 ppm and 94.6% at 10 ppm as maximum inhibition efficiency after 2 and 24 h immersion, respectively. Activation energy studies, XPS analysis, and isotherm studies show that the inhibition mechanism occurs through physical interaction. These results show an excellent alternative for using pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima), an agroindustry waste, as a corrosion inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel (MS) is one of the most used materials in the industry, its low cost, excellent mechanical properties, and recyclability make it the ideal candidate for several applications. In pipeline industries, mild steel is considered standard, and the material can be flexed or bent without losing its structural integrity [1–3]. The oil industry uses pipes to transport oil, gas, water, and other fluids. However, the transport of these products ends up favoring corrosion processes as well as encrustation (oxide formation), due to the acidity and/or salinity of the medium. The most used process for cleaning pipes is acid pickling, with hydrochloric acid being the most used acid due to its relatively low cost and ability to form highly soluble metal chlorides [1,3,4].

Although necessary and efficient, this process will favor the corrosion process and the generation of gaseous hydrogen that can permeate into the metal. These combined processes generate astronomical losses for the industry, which makes it essential to use a methodology that can inhibit the corrosion process.

The use of corrosion inhibitors is already well disseminated and established in the literature and industrial use. Inhibitor molecules act by adsorbing on the metal surface, creating a protective layer between the metal and the corrosive environment, thus blocking the active corrosion sites [5–7]. For an inhibitor molecule to be considered a good corrosion inhibitor, it must present in its chemical structure heteroatoms such as N, S, P, O, multiple bonds, and aromatic rings that can interact with the metallic surface in a physical and/or chemical way [5,8].

From this point of view, carbon quantum dots are excellent candidates for corrosion inhibitors. These carbon particles have excellent properties such as facile and easy preparation, low cost and nontoxicity, good water solubility, excellent chemical stability, and are rich in functional groups, multiple bonds, and aromatic rings, are highly soluble and are easily modified with heteroatoms, that makes carbon quantum dots excellent electron donor [8–13]. Carbon quantum dots can be obtained by bottom-up and top-down methods and have recently been studied as corrosion inhibitors in various media with very satisfactory results [4,10,14–20]. However, few studies have addressed using carbon quantum dots obtained from biomass waste as a corrosion inhibitor.

Some research groups have developed methods for obtaining CQDs derived from biowaste [21–25]. The use of this methodology becomes quite interesting from an environmental point of view. With population growth, food production has increased more and more, which has generated an increasing amount of agro-industrial waste [26–30]. Using this raw material adds value to a product often without application and, at the same time can improve the product obtained. The green raw materials are already rich in O, N, P, and S, depending on their origin, favoring the endogenous self-doping, which helps in the adsorption process.

Pumpkin is cultivated worldwide for various applications such as medicinal, commercial, decorative, and mainly food use [31–33]. In 2017, China and India together produced approximately 13 million tons of pumpkin [34]. However, waste is produced during its processing, mainly the peels and seeds. Despite being considered agroindustry waste, the seed is rich in carbon, with several bioactive molecules in its composition, rich in phenolic compounds, proteins, unsaturated fatty acids, saponins, and vitamins [32,33,35]. This compositional characteristic makes pumpkin seeds a great alternative as a raw material to produce CQDs; in addition to the high carbon content, they contain several biomolecules in their composition that can favor endogenous self-doping.

Therefore, this paper aims to produce sulfur, nitrogen, and phosphorus carbon quantum dot, named in this work as N,P,S-CQDs, from pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima) and their application as a corrosion green inhibitor for mild steel in 1 mol L−1 hydrochloric acid medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus carbon quantum dot synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize carbon quantum dots, 5 g of pumpkin seeds (Cucurbita maxima), previously roasted and crushed, were added to a synthesis flask with 5 mL of H3PO4 85 % (purchased from ISOFAR). The mixture was placed in a silicone bath and refluxed at 200 °C for 8 h. After that, 100 mL of water were added to the resulting mixture and filtered through 45 and 0.22 μm membranes to remove the pumpkin solid residues and large particulates, respectively. The resulting solution was then subjected to the dialysis process using a membrane with a size of 1 kDa for 48 h to remove impurities; during dialysis, water was changed more than 3 times a day. After 48 h, the solution was placed to dry in the oven, and then the stock solution was prepared at a concentration of 100 ppm.

The morphology of the obtained N,P,S-CQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). The TEM images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) chemical analysis were acquired using a probe-corrected FEI Titan 80–300 TEM working at 80 kV to reduce beam damaged in the sample. The images were acquired in scanning TEM (STEM) mode with a high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) detector. For better contrast level, stability under the electron beam, and visualization of the N,P,S-CQDs, the specimens were prepared by drop-coating the N,P,S-CQDs [36] dispersion onto a graphene oxide-coated 300-mesh lacey carbon copper grid. The crystallinity was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer (Cu Kαλ = 0.1542 nm), fixed energy source (40 kV and 20 mA) and a scanning rate of 0.02° s−1 at 2θ intervals from 5° to 80°, the samples were prepared as thin films on glass slides, by drop-casting and room-temperature drying, the interplanar distance was calculated using the Bragg's equation (Equation (1)). Raman spectra were recorded on a Confocal Raman Microscope: SENTERRA II with 532 nm laser excitation.

The particles' size was also analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using Anton Paar and a 0.2 mg mL−1 of N,P,S-CQDs in DMF dispersion. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS), and X-ray excited photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were used to evaluate the composition of the particles. A Nicolet spectrometer, model 6700FT-IR, was used for the FTIR analysis. The samples were prepared in KBr, and the infrared spectrum was measured in the range between 400 and 4000 cm−1. The XPS analysis was performed in an ultra-high vacuum equipment (ScientaOmicron) using a non-monochromatic Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) X-ray source, operated at 15 kV and 20 mA. The spectra sweep (survey) were acquired in the range of 1100 eV–0 eV, with a step of 1.0 eV, acquisition time of 0.3 s, and energy step of the analyzer of 160 eV. The C1s, N1s, O1s, P2p and S2p high-resolution spectra were obtained with an analyzer pass energy of 30 eV. The absorbance spectrum of a 0.2 mg mL−1 N,P,S-CQDs in water was determined in the range of 500 to 200 nm using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu).

Where,

For the corrosion tests, mild steel with a composition of C: 0.18, P: 0.04, S: 0.05, Mn: 0.30, Si: trace, and Fe: balance was used as the study material. Before each test, the steel was submitted to a surface preparation process that consisted of polishing using sandpapers with different granulometries (100, 320 and 600 mesh) followed by cleaning with deionized water and acetone. For the mass loss tests, the specimen area used was approximately 13 cm2, while for the electrochemical studies, the exposed area was approximately 0.8 cm2.

The mild steel plates were immersed in the acid solution (1.0 mol L−1 HCl) with and without inhibitor for 2 and 24 h at room temperature. The inhibitor concentrations evaluated were 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 ppm. The performance of the inhibitor over other time intervals was studied in the presence of 10 ppm for 2, 5, 24 and 48 h. Before being immersed, the plates were weighed, and their dimensions measured with the aid of a pachymeter. The variation in the mass of the plates before and after each test was used to calculate the corrosion rate (equation (2)) and then the corrosion inhibition efficiency (equation (3)).

The gravimetric assay is also important for studying the effect of temperature and activated thermodynamic parameters on the inhibitory process. Thus, fixing the concentration at 10 ppm, the test was carried out varying, 25, 35, 45, and 55 °C. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency were also obtained by equations (2) and (3), respectively. The corrosion rate (W

The electrochemical tests were carried out in a classic 3-electrodes cell, with the calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, platinum as the counter electrode, and the mild steel plate as the working electrode. Assays were conducted using the AUTOLAB potentiostat. All tests were conducted under natural conditions of aeration and at a temperature around 25 °C in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, at concentrations of 10, 20, 40, and 60 ppm.

The open circuit potential (OCP) was recorded for 2 h to reach a stable state. Electrochemical impedance measurements were acquired using a 10 mV amplitude sinusoidal wave in the frequency from 105 to 10−2 Hz and the polarization curves were acquired from −300 to +300 V vs. OCP with a scan rate of 1 mV s−1.

The charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) was used as a parameter to evaluate the inhibition efficiency in the impedance studies. The inhibition efficiency was calculated by equation (4).

The surface morphology of mild steel samples was evaluated by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) (Magellan 450) working at 10 kV and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) NanoWizard JPK. In all tests, the mild steel plate was studied after 2 h immersion test in 0.1 mol L−1 HCl in the presence and absence of the inhibitor.

To obtain carbon quantum dots with good inhibitory efficiency, different synthesis times were studied (2, 4, and 8 h). The gravimetric test was used to obtain the inhibition efficiency. The acid volume and temperature were fixed at 200 °C (due to experimental limitations) and 5 mL (the required amount of concentrated acid to suspend the biomass), respectively. In these tests, 10 ppm of N,P,S-CQDs were used.

As can be seen in Table 1 , the highest corrosion efficiency was obtained for the 8-h synthesis time. The greater efficiency for the longer reaction time may be related to a longer biomass hydrolysis time, allowing a longer reaction time for the formation of quantum dots and the introduction of functional groups of the constituents present in the sample and also of the phosphoric acid itself that may be introducing into the structure of CQDs phosphorus-related groups [37].

The STEM micrography (Fig. 1 ) clearly shows that the carbon quantum dots were obtained from the acid hydrolysis at 200 °C from pumpkin seeds. In Fig. 1a, a graphene oxide flake is observed, encompassed by a network formed by N,P,S-CQDs. To visualize individual N,P,S-CQDs, we explore a region of low concentration. Even in this reduced concentration zone, quantum dots persist, forming small clusters, as highlighted by arrows in Fig. 1b, with a size distribution consistently below 10 nm. EDS analysis conducted on larger agglomerates (images not presented) reveals the presence of C, N, O, and P in the synthesized material. This suggests inherent self-doping, a phenomenon that will be further investigated through FTIR and XPS techniques.

The size of N,P,S-CQDs was also analyzed by DLS, and as can be seen from Fig. 2 , the particles showed a hydrodynamic size distribution of less than 10 nm (average of 3.5 nm), corroborating what was observed in the TEM analyses.

Fig. 3 a shows the diffraction profile of the particles, showing that it has a single broad diffraction peak centered around 23°, which is attributed to the turbostratic graphitic carbon phase [38,39]. The peak broadening has been attributed to the small size of the particles, while the low intensity shows the amorphous characteristic of the material [39]. The interplanar distance, calculated from equation (1), was 0.38 nm greater than the interplanar distance of bulk graphite (0.33 nm), possibly due to the presence of functional groups in the particle structure [40]. The Raman spectra of N,P,S-CQDs (Fig. 3b) present the G and D bands, originating from the stretching of the sp2 carbon atom and structural defects/disorders in the carbon lattice, respectively [41]. The G band indicates the presence of well-ordered carbon structures, and the D band indicates the presence of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. Typical characteristic of CQD, where a spherical core can exhibit multiple-layer graphite structures connected with surface groups [42]. The morphological characterization indicates that the CQDs were successfully obtained, and it is necessary to study the chemical composition of the CQDs.

Photoluminescence is a very important property of N,P,S-CQDs, in Fig. 4 , the spectrum presents a well-defined peak around 280 nm, with a tail elongation from 400 nm. The peak at 280 nm is attributed to the π – π * transition of the aromatic π system, similar to of the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, while the elongation can be attributed to the n−π* transitions of heteroatom (N and O)-containing bonds [43,44].

The chemical and structural information of the N,P,S-CQDs provided by the FTIR can be seen in Fig. 5 . The FTIR spectrum presents several peaks that give us information about the composition of the material. Strong and broad peaks at 3430 and 3200 cm−1 indicate the presence of O–H and N–H stretching vibration, respectively, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and/or amine groups on the particle's surface [45–47]. Also, stretching vibrations of C–H, C–N, and N–H are observed around 2960, 1558, and 1400 cm−1, respectively [46,48,49]. The peak at 1655 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C and C=O [50]. The presence of phosphorus can be confirmed by the presence of peaks around 2416, 1153, 1060, and 947 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of P–H, P=O, P–O, and P–O–C, respectively [46,47,51]. Finally, stretching vibrations of C–S is observed at around 748 cm−1 [48]. The FTIR analysis, in addition to confirming the presence of several hydrophilic groups, which are of extreme importance in the solubility of the inhibitor, also confirms the presence of S, N, and P in N,P,S-CQDs.

The XPS technique was used to determine the elemental composition of the material. As can be seen in Fig. 6 a, according to the analysis, the produced carbon quantum dot presents in its structure the elements N, S, P, C, and O observed by the peaks around 401.6, 168, 134.6, 285.6, and 532.6 eV that are characteristic of the binding energy of N1s, S2p, P2p, C1s, and Os1, respectively [52–55]. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum shows 5 peaks at 284.0, 284.9, 285.9 287.4, and 289.0 eV, indicating the presence of C=C/C–S, C–C/C–H, C–O/C–P/C–N, C=O and COOH, respectively (Fig. 6b) [55–58]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s shows the presence of O–N/P/S, O=P, and O=C bonds represented by peaks 529.6, 531.1, and 532.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 6c) [56,58,59]. The N 1s spectra identify two peaks at 399.6 and 401.6 that can be attributed to the N–H and graphitic N bonds, respectively (Fig. 6d) [55,59]. Both P 2p and S 2p spectra show a single peak at 133.7 (P 2p) and 168.7 eV (S 2p), which are characteristic of P–C [56,59] and C–SO3 [58] bonds, respectively (Fig. 6e and 6f).

These results corroborate the EDS and FTIR analysis indicating the presence of N, S, P and O in the structure of N,P,S-CQDs and the presence of functional groups based on these elements on their surface. The EDS analysis does not show the presence of sulfur. This could be attributed to the low sulfur content in the N,P,S-CQDs and the quantity of the sample used for analysis. Through the characterization study carried out, it is possible to state that endogenous self-doping N,P,S-CQDs concerning N and S was successfully obtained from pumpkin seeds. N, S, and O possibly come from organic compounds present in pumpkin seeds, such as carbohydrates, proteins, saponins, and fiber [32,60–62]. Although pumpkin seeds have a high content of the element phosphorus by the presence of the phytic acid [60], it is not possible to state that the presence of P came only from biomass; the element P could have been introduced by the addition of H3PO4 [37].

Table 2 shows the immersion test results at 2 and 24 h in the absence and in the presence of the inhibitor at concentrations of 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 ppm in 1.0 mol L −1 of HCl. For both times studied, the corrosion rate decreases significantly with the successive addition of the inhibitor, indicating that over time, a protective layer is formed on the steel surface. Evaluating only the period of 2 h, the maximum efficiency of 91 % was reached for the concentrations of 60 and 80 ppm, indicating that there was a saturation of inhibitory molecules from the concentration of 60 ppm. For 24 h immersion time, the saturation already happens from 10 ppm, reaching a maximum efficiency of 95%. As from 60 ppm, there was saturation, and the electrochemical tests were performed in the range of 10–60 ppm.

To better understand the behavior of the inhibitor adsorption as a function of time, based on the results obtained at 2 and 24 h, an additional efficiency study was carried out over time using a concentration of 10 ppm at 2, 5, 24, and 48 (Fig. 7 ). A concentration of 10 ppm was chosen because, as shown in Table 2, for this concentration in 24 h, maximum efficiency is already observed, and as the concentration of the inhibitor is directly linked to corrosion efficiency, using the maximum concentration could mask a possible degradation as a function of time, due to excess inhibitor in the corrosive medium.

As shown in Fig. 7, over time, there is an increase in the IE until 24 h, reaching 95%, showing the stability of the N,P,S-CQDs. Evaluating the adsorption kinetics, we observed that the adsorption of the inhibitor needs time to reach the maximum inhibition efficiency. These results show the potential use of the inhibitor for systems where the MS is in prolonged contact with the corrosive medium.

The gravimetric essays with temperature variation are very important not only to evaluate the effect of temperature on inhibition but also to provide us with important parameters about the thermodynamics activation parameters of the corrosion process in the absence and presence of the inhibitor. The essay was conducted by setting the concentration at 10 ppm and varying the temperature between 25 and 55 °C, as can be seen in Table 3 .

The results presented in the table show that the corrosion rate increases with the temperature for both uninhibited and inhibited systems for all temperatures studied and the presence of the inhibitor decreases the corrosion rate. However, is observed that the inhibitor had its protective efficiency impaired at 55 °C, where the inhibition efficiency decreased. The interactions between the carbon quantum dots and mild steel surface can occur in a physical, chemical, or mixed form, with physics being the weakest among them [63]. To study what kind of interaction is taking place, using the data shown in Table 3, the Arrhenius equation was used [64] (Equation (6)), where it is possible to calculate the activation energy by plotting the corrosion rate vs the inverse of the temperature (Fig. 8 ).
(6)
log
W = log
A −
E a
2.303
R T
Where
W
is the corrosion rate,
A
is the Arrhenius constant,
E a
is the activation energy,
R
is the gas constant, and
T
is temperature.

The activation energy obtained in the presence of the inhibitor was 52.1 ± 1.67 kJ, while in its absence, it was 49.0 ± 0.24 kJ. These results suggest that the N,P,S-CQDs interact with the MS surface physically through electrostatic interactions [65].

To carry out electrochemical tests, the system must be in a stationary state so that the influence of the inhibitor can be assessed. The open circuit potential versus immersion time graph (Fig. 9 ) shows that at a time of 7200 s, all systems (in the presence and absence of the inhibitor) reach a stationary OCP, showing a displacement from the blank to more anodic potentials for all inhibitor concentrations, displaying a more pronounced inhibitory effect on anodic reactions.

Fig. 10 shows the Nyquist (Fig. 10A), impedance modulus (Fig. 10B), and Bode plots (Fig. 10C) obtained by the electrochemical test. As can be seen, in the absence of an inhibitor, the diagram shows only one capacitive loop related to the charge transfer resistance and the capacitance of the electrical double layer. With the addition of the inhibitor, an inductive loop appears, whose increase in its concentration causes an increase in both inductive and capacitive loops, indicating once again that the inhibitor was able to form a protective film on the surface of the steel, hindering the action of the H+ ions.

This inductive loop could be linked to the relaxation of species like (FeH)ads, and/or FeOHads on the electrode surface, both intermediaries of cathodic and anodic reactions, which may interact with inhibitory species, then being able to influence the inhibition efficiency of the extract [63,66,67]. The origin of this inductive loop could also be an interaction of inhibitory species on the free surface of mild steel.

The impedance modulus increases at low frequencies with N,P,S-CQDs concentration (Fig. 10B), indicating that the higher the N,P,S-CQDs concentration, the more particles are adsorbing on the surface of the MS, increasing the surface coverage degree, which will hinder the charge transfer process between the mild steel and the solution. The maximum phase angle (Fig. 10C) increases significantly in the presence of N,P,S-CQDs, however, it remains quite constant with N,P,S-CQDs concentration, achieving a value close to 60°, suggesting that a protective film was formed by the increase of the capacitive behavior.

Table 4 translates what has been seen in Fig. 10; the increase in the concentration of the inhibitor causes a decrease in the capacitance of the electrical double layer (Cdl) and, consequently an increase in the charge transfer resistance. The Cdl represents the accumulation of charge that occurs between the electrolyte and the metal, which will directly affect the ability of the material to exchange charge with the environment, represented by the Rct. Its decrease indicates that the inhibitor, when adsorbed on the metal surface, forms a protective layer preventing the H+ ions from attacking the steel, thus protecting it from corrosion. The Table also provides us with information about the inductive, which increases with increasing inhibitor concentration, showing that this parameter has a direct connection with the inhibitor.
An electrochemical equivalent circuit (Fig. 11 ) was used in the simulation using Nova 2.1.4 software to determine electrochemical parameters.
Where Rs is the solution resistance, Rf and CPE are the resistance and capacitance of the adsorbed inhibitor, and L and RL are the components of the inductive elements.
Finally, electrochemical polarization was performed, an important tool to understand the corrosion mechanism since the inhibitor can act by inhibiting the cathodic or anodic reaction or both.

Fig. 12 shows, once again, the effect of the inhibitor on the corrosion process; the addition of C-QDs causes a drop in both anodic and cathodic current densities, probably by the adsorption by the inhibitory species hindering the action of H+. The graph also shows that the addition of the inhibitor inhibits both the anodic and cathodic reactions, thus being a mixed type of inhibitor, and moreover, it was able to shift the corrosion potential to more positive potentials, as observed with the OCP. Through these curves, the extrapolation of the Tafel curve was performed, with results shown in Table 5 .

Table 5 shows that the successive addition of the inhibitor shifts the corrosion potential to more positive potentials, and the current density also reduces considerably, reaching a maximum efficiency of 92% for 60 ppm. It is also possible to observe that the inhibitor could inhibit both the anodic and cathodic reactions, confirming that it is a mixed-type inhibitor [68,69].

Isotherms are widely used and provide us with information about the mechanism of the adsorption process, like a number of inhibitor molecules adsorbed, formed layers, and adsorption equilibrium constant, among others. The isotherms used in this work were Langmuir [70], Temkin [70], Flory-Huggins [70], and El-Awady [70] (Equations (5)–(8), respectively).

Where C is the concentration of the inhibitor (mg L−1), K is the constant equilibrium of adsorption, a is the lateral interaction parameter between the adsorbed molecules, x is the number of adsorbed water molecules replaced by the inhibitor molecules, and y is the number of inhibitor molecules adsorbed in each active site. θ is the degree of surface coverage by the inhibitor molecule.
Using the corrosion inhibition efficiency data obtained in the gravimetric test (Table 2), curves were plotted for all models whose equations obtained from linear regression can be seen in Table 6 .

Of all the models studied, considering the determination coefficient, the model that best suited was the Temkin model. However, all correlation coefficients obtained for the other models were higher than 0.99 and cannot be ignored. The Langmuir model assumes that the interaction between the inhibitor molecule and metal surface occurs by the formation of a monolayer with equivalent active sites and single-molecular adsorption without lateral interaction. However, an angular coefficient lower than 1 can be observed for the Langmuir model, indicating a deviation of the ideality of the model [70]. Then, some information can also be taken from the other isotherms.

Temkin's model gives us information about the nature of the interaction in the adsorbed layer. The parameter “a” can be obtained through the equation of the model, representing the molecular interaction. The value of a is negative, indicating that in the adsorbed layer, there is repulsion between the inhibitors [70].

The Flory-Huggins model is an important tool to evaluate when the Lamgmuir model deviates from ideality. Through this model, it is possible to discover the number of adsorbed water molecules replaced by the inhibitor molecules (represented by x). The value of x obtained was 2.143, indicating that one inhibitory molecule replaces 2.143 water molecules [70].

Finally, the El-Awady model tells us the number of molecules occupying an active site on the metal, a parameter represented by y. The value obtained by the 1/y model was 2.004, indicating that the inhibitory molecules are bulky [70].

These results are expected when dealing with quantum dots, since the uncontrolled synthesis; it cannot be said that each CQD is identical. Another factor that must be considered is that quantum dots are complex particles with several groups that can interact with the mild steel surface, as seen in the characterization results. So, the interactions between the MS surface and different active sites of the inhibitory molecule are already expected. Besides, since the purity of the inhibitor as well as its molar mass are unknown, the ΔG calculation is not possible.

In Fig. 13 , it is possible to observe the MS surface after surface preparation (Fig. 13A and B), after 2 h immersion in 1 mol L−1 of HCl (Fig. 13C and D), and after 2 h immersion in 1.0 mol L−1 of HCl in the presence of 60 ppm of the inhibitor (Fig. 13E and F). As can be seen, before being subjected to corrosion tests, the MS surface has a smooth surface with the presence of some abrading lines. When subjected to acid medium in the absence of the inhibitor, the steel surface is transformed, presenting a surface full of roughness (Fig. 13C and D), probably caused by the dissolution of iron by hydrochloric acid. When compared with the MS samples submitted to the acid medium in the presence of the inhibitor (Fig. 13E and F), the steel surface is almost equal to the MS surface after surface treatment (Fig. 13A and B).

The AFM image (Fig. 14 ) elucidates what was observed in the SEM analyses; after being subjected to an acidic medium (Fig. 14B), there is a dissolution of iron which leads to an increase in surface roughness, having a rms of ±88 nm, when the inhibitor is added (Fig. 14C) a rms of ±25 nm is obtained while for the freshly treated MS, the roughness obtained was ±20 nm. These surface results corroborate the electrochemical results, showing that the inhibitor could mitigate MS corrosion in 1 mol L−1 of HCl.

Through XPS analysis (Fig. 15 ), the survey spectrum of the mild steel in the absence (Fig. 15A) and the presence of the inhibitor (Fig. 15B) was obtained after 2 h immersion in 1 mol L−1 HCl. As can be seen, in the presence of the inhibitor, the spectrum shows the presence of nitrogen, indicating that the inhibitor is adsorbed on the MS plate since the same element cannot be observed in the specimen that was not submitted to the test in the presence of the inhibitor. After the Fe 2p deconvolution for both plates (Fig. 15C and D), it is possible to observe that the MS with the adsorbed inhibitor does not present Fe bonds with N, S, and P; this is an indication that the inhibitor interacts through physical interactions, corroborating studies of activation energy.

It is important to highlight that the XPS analysis is a very important tool, however, it is susceptible to the amount of inhibitor adsorbed on the steel as well as the region of analysis. Probably because of this, the elements S and P, which are part of the chemical composition of the inhibitor, were not found.

Through the characterization analyses, the carbon quantum dots obtained from pumpkin seeds have a size distribution of less than 10 nm and several functional groups in their structure according to the XPS and FTIR results. The particle size, chemical composition, and high surface energy favor particle self-aggregation caused by van der Waals interactions between the protonated groups [70,71]. Considering the immersion study as a function of time carried out, as adsorption does not occur quickly, the particles will have enough time to self-aggregate, and due to the presence of protonated functional groups, these agglomerates will interact electrostatically with the Cl− adsorbed on the mild steel surface forming physical interaction [72]. This interaction is based on the activation energy value found by the Arrhenius equation.

In this work, carbon quantum dots were successfully produced from pumpkin seed, presenting a high amount of OH groups that guarantee a hydrophilic character, which is extremely important for corrosion inhibitors, and the presence of atoms like S, N, and P that improve the interaction between the inhibitor and the MS. The inhibitor showed good inhibition efficiency using low amounts, reaching 91% efficiency in 2 h of immersion with 60 ppm and 95% in 24 h with only 10 ppm. The N,P,S-CQDs obtained from pumpkin seed adsorb physically on the MS surface, inhibiting both anodic and cathodic reactions. XPS analysis showed the presence of the inhibitor on the MS surface and MEV analysis corroborated the inhibitory action of the N,P,S-CQDs towards the MS corrosion in 1 mol L−1 HCl solution.

These results show the versatility of the inhibitor produced and the potential for biomass to be used as a raw material to produce carbon quantum dots. Not only were CQDs obtained from biomass, but a material with endogenous self-doping was obtained, a very important factor for the material. Doping CQDs is extremely important as it gives it different properties or even intensifies it, increasing the material's range of application. Addressing not only the problematic issues of corrosion but also economic and environmental issues.
